speaker: interviewer
text: Tape Number four. September 28, 1995. The survivor is Regina Gaska. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were talking about when you were in Leipzig. You were liberated. The rabbi came and told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Told us all to bathe. And he said Kaddish. And we stayed there for a while until he came and told us we have to leave, and told us which way you want to go? So you going to the American side or to the Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought that you were with the Americans already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I'd been in Leipzig. Was that time still they say it's going to be the Russian they are liberated. So we didn't want to stay with the Russian. We heard stories about them. So we choose to walk the other side. So we stayed. A group of us-- five of us girls stayed together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Frankfurt am Main, to Germany. We didn't go on. We stayed in Frankfurt am Main. So was a Jewish committee already. And they locate us in a hotel. We stayed five girls in a hotel together in one room. And we slept there until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got before the Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to find your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to register for family and look for relatives or something. Nothing was showing up. So I met my husband there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barn of the [INAUDIBLE] in the station where the trains come in. They gave us meals there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in the train station? That's what-- they were giving you meals?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. The Jewish committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Either this was from the Germans or the Jewish committee. No, I think was from the Germans that served us meals.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- who-- the Germans were serving you meals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if it was the Germans or the Jewish committee. I really don't know. So we stayed in the hotel and went down for meals. I met my husband

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you met him?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was something in May. Something-- I don't exactly the date-- I don't need to remember. I don't remember dates at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were liberated at the end of April? April 26th, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the first time you saw him? Your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We saw him. He stayed in, you know, a job from that place until May, and talked to-- you know. Then he started to write me little notes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of notes was he writing you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of notes, you know. He'd write me notes. And he lived in Asschafenburg, and he traveled all the way to Frankfurt. And the trains wasn't running that time. It was on coal trains, he came. He was all black from the coal trains. He says, he came all the time. I figured maybe he likes me. So it went back and forth. Then he moved to Frankfurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was coming for a while just to visit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All the time. Yes. He stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the notes that he was writing you, what was he talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talk all kind of things. Like a girl and a boyfriend talking, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was courting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looks like. And we got married in Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take him until he proposed?

speaker: interviewee
text: He proposed-- it didn't take a month. He was alone. I was alone. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when he asked you to marry him, what he said?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I don't remember this thing. It's funny. It's-- we made plans we're going to marry in November.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your friends say, who you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my friends were having boyfriends, still. And they got married-- one got married before us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were like the second wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second wedding. Yeah. The other one was the first wedding. They all got married. So we figured we'd get married. Nothing to eat, but we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wedding like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't ask. Terrible. It was nice for us, that time, yeah. My cousin of mine worked in the American army and they gave out rations. So we stole some rations for my wedding. Could you imagine? And a German gave us apples. And another German gave us-- that is a friend-- he was half-Jewish. She gave us liquor and this was the wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were at the wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a dozen. The rabbi came to the house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear?

speaker: interviewee
text: We bought a dress for a couple pieces of bread. A bride dress. We sewed the dress there. And we bought a pair of shoes, brown shoes. We didn't have white shoes. And this was the wedding. The dress went to one girl, to the other girls. And the picture was taken in a broken building, as I remember. Was a beautiful night. A cold night, but beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel the day you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt good because I had somebody for myself. I'm not alone. We still looked around for family and things. And after this, I got very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? I was sick. I lost a child, and I got poisoned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of poisoning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. And I was in the hospital for three months. They didn't think I'm going to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was right after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was very sick. I was in the hospital so long. I was all poisoned. They didn't believe I'm going to make it. And husband stood by me, all the time came to the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One friend of ours-- I needed penicillin to save my life, so one friend registered. He's sick. He needs penicillin. And he gave the penicillin to me to save my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel? I mean, you'd been through so much and now you lost a baby and you were sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know. Maybe that's the punishment because I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find any family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Survivors, no. I found family in the United States. My father had a sister here. He had a brother here. Although I didn't know the address, I knew he had a sister and a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to the United States. How long were you in Germany for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in a DP Camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] We stayed in [INAUDIBLE]. And we moved to Frankfurt am Main before we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing? Were you working in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your husband doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it adjusting to being free to everything you've gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, adjusting wasn't too bad because-- although you still look at in the back to see if soldiers are walking over you. You know, you're bound free. I still get hear the clapping of the shoes, the knocking of the shoes this day. But still I wasn't free. I'm still not free. At night, when I woke by myself, I still look in the back. Soldier walking there. Still not free. The nights are terrible. I still scream. Most of the night, I wake up screaming or I'm moaning,

speaker: interviewee
text: And my husband screams, you're going to give me a heart attack with this sleep. And I says, I cannot do it. I cannot help myself. What do you want from me? So came to the United States. I suffered a lot. You came here in '49?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? Coming to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coming to this country was nice. My family wasn't so nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? Why weren't they nice? What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they say? I came back from the boat. My cousin picked us up. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had some luggage. They told us to keep the luggage outside. I said, why? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe it's lice. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I opened my luggage and I showed them it's not lice. And my luggage cannot go in, so I'm not coming in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, the luggage went in. I made my bed. They gave me a room upstairs. They told me not to sleep with my husband. Why did they say that? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK, keep going. Yeah. Not to sleep because she's religious. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I said, no. My husband says, I'm sleeping with my wife. She didn't believe he's Jewish, because he didn't [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I got pregnant, and I heard them talking, here you got yourself trouble. She's not going to leave the house. She's expecting a baby. The baby's going to [YIDDISH] on the floor. They got a-- they wanted to get rid of me. They looked for apartment. Couldn't find an apartment. Finally we got a furnished room. We lived on Glenmore Avenue in Brooklyn, in a furnished room with Mrs. Taylor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to-- we moved in there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the house was-- I didn't know what cockroaches is. There were so many.

speaker: interviewer
text: I called my cousin up, and I said, [INAUDIBLE], it's so many things. It's just good luck. To me, it looked like spiders. I didn't know what cockroaches is. Just with spiders and slugs. How much long do I need? So she came to that and visit. And She says it's not. It's something different. It's cockroaches. Started to clean, work in the house, I lost the baby. In that time, everything went down the hill losing babies. I lost nine babies-- 10 babies. I had 10 pregnancies, one living. When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in 1959. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After fourteen years of marriage, she was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been a wonderful moment when your daughter was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. With problems, this one, this one, this one. I didn't believe it, I could have a baby, with the screaming at night, with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born. We was very happy. It was the happiest days of our life. She was a good kid. Smart kid. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wherever she went to school, she did excellent. And now she's married and has children, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has her own children. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spent much time in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, we spent a month. First time going to Israel, we spent a month. In that, we had [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt very lonely because everybody had family-- like, Shabbas came along and we were invited to family. We weren't invited. We didn't have nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my daughter felt terrible, because, where's my grandparents? Where's my uncles? And I told her. We made up uncles. We made up relatives. But then we had to tell her the truth. We have nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't understand. And still we didn't talk about it. I don't think that she knows what we went through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When she complains, she's tired. She has the babies. I say, I don't blame you. I couldn't complain to nobody when I was tired and I came home to an empty house. She doesn't understand this. And I don't think that they understand what we've been through. I really don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel-- do you feel that the way-- everything you went through affected the way you raised your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm very protective of her, you know? Watch her. She doesn't like this. I don't think so. She's different. She tells me things. I wasn't raised nice for instance. We didn't nurse her. I says, that time wasn't the style to nurse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like she would say, you're too strict. Want your mother too strict, but I was strict with her. I was afraid. Was the only child. We didn't have other children. I had another child after of my daughter. She lived just five days. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the hospital made a mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the hospital make a mistake?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my daughter had this first baby, we told them our story. The baby was born normal. Everything normal. And then developed yellow jaundice. So they tell me she died from yellow jaundice. So when I talked with the doctor when he delivered Renee's, my daughter's, babies, he says, babies don't die from yellow jaundice. You must catch a germ in the hospital. That's why she died. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel you've gotten through so much tragedy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I still don't believe it. I'm still suffering from this. At night, I think maybe, maybe I did something wrong. Maybe because I survived, that's why I'm suffering so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: I blame myself. Why me? Why do I have to survive? I didn't pay. Because every time, I came home to empty house. You prepare everything. You figured, oh, now, you're going to have a baby and you come home. It's nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last one, I almost lost my life. I got 14 pint of blood. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's hard to tell somebody. You have nobody to complain. I cannot complain to my husband. He suffers the same as I do. He was a good man. When I was very sick, he stood by me, day and night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very sick in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does it feel now to have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Feels wonderful. But they don't-- it's different. You want to give them something. Don't give them this. They're different. You have to stay away and let them live their own life. It's different here. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do want to give today? Why did you want to do this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is, somebody should know what happened in the war. It is not a lie. People didn't go to the crematorium by themselves. Like they say they went into the crematorium by themself. That's not true. They tell them they're going to get a bath. They're going to be washed. Bring the clothes together. You come out. You would have the clothes wrapped, you shouldn't have to mix up. Nobody believe what's going to happen, this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't go like chicken to the slaughter. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's hard when people tells you this is not true. It's made up. You see it on television, what it's doing now with the swastikas all over. This boils my blood. I can't take it no more. I don't watch it no more. I turn the television off. Of course, at night the bed is moving. It's jumping. I'm screaming. And Sam is getting sick from it, too. We're both getting sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that this testimony today is in memorial for your family who didn't make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Remember the names. Who's going to remember the names? My daughter's going to keep tradition. She'll remember the names, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names? You want to close by mentioning the names of your family, your parents, your brothers, and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the memory. Who else is going to remember them? Nobody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names? Your family's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest is Israel, Sarah, Shlomo, Josh, and Toby. And my brother's grandchildren-- his wife was [? Lieber. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot the name. What is? [? Leia, ?] [? Yankele, ?] [? Ishael. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kalman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gerzymowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I got this picture from my aunt in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In United States. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's my mother. And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Brucha. And her family name is Cittrin, her maiden name. And the baby is my oldest brother, and his name is Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Miedzyrzecz. And I found the picture by my aunt in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your brother here? This is the oldest brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is my brother Shlomo. Next to Shlomo is my mother. Next to my mother is my sister, Sarah. Next to Sarah is my father. And next to my father is the youngest brother, Josh. And my mother is holding the baby, Toby. And I'm next to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around how old were you, here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six? Five? Who knows? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken after the liberation, a couple months later.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel when you look at these photos of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe it's really my family. I can't believe it, that's-- I'm the only who could tell if it's my family. I think it's like telling a story from a book. Do I really had a mother and father? How come nobody's there? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture taken after my wedding in November, 1945. November the 16th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talked a little bit about when this was-- how you got the dress, how you bought some bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we borrowed the dress for some bread from a friend. And the dress was handed down and everybody got the same dress and got married after the liberation. We all shared the dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Frankfurt you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankfurt am Main, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All right. This is a picture taken a couple months after I came out of the hospital, when I was very sick. You could see I still have-- not all my [INAUDIBLE]. I was thin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Frankfurt? You and your husband, Sam?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my daughter. She was born in 1959, November the fourth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Renee Barbara. Named after both mothers. After my husband's mother and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was a very wanted baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a pictures taken in Israel. I was there-- 1970, I think-- I was there in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is you and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was, this particular picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's close by the [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken at my daughter's wedding with her husband. His name is Mark Saldon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They married in March 28, 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Temple, what is it? I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the two grandchildren of ours. One is Jennifer Sarah. And the other one is Meredith Ashley.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jennifer's going to be two in October. And this one is six-- going to be-- on Sunday. They give--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second. Just going to put the camera--

speaker: interviewee
text: We love them very much. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is-- after that the liberation, they gave us these cards to carry with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was from the committee of liberated Jews in Frankfurt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The registration card.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Jewish committee gave it to us. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think we got this because of the meals that they served meals. We had to show this card. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my family. This is my husband. Alden, the granddaughter, the oldest, she is going to be three in October. In the back of me is my daughter, Renee, holding the baby who's going to be six weeks old on Sunday. And this is my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sam, would you like to say a few words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is Jennifer. This is Barney. This is Meredith. She is named after my father. [CRYING] And this is my wife. We have been married 50 years coming November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're both survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Both survivors. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Barney's there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, Barney-- look, Jennifer, Barney's here. You see it. See? Barney's here. Barney's here. OK? Can you give Grandpa a kiss? Give Grandpa a kiss. Give Grandpa a kiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've had a lot of-- and Sam, you've had a lot of hardship even here? And so I know how much your daughter and your grandchildren mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: They mean a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Renee, do you want to say a few words?

speaker: interviewee
text: The person that's not here is my husband, Mark, who couldn't be here today because he couldn't get off work. He took off a lot of time when this one was born. And we're just glad to be here and help, you know, my parents on this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about being a child of survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: At times, it was tough. I mean, not having grandparents around the holidays, you felt it. Especially Thanksgiving and Christmas vacation, when everyone would go visit Grandma and you didn't. But it also made a closer family when you were together, you know? We relied on each other, because there was no one else.

words.text: because there was no one else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say? Mrs. Gaska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all we have to say. That's all my family. That's all I have.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome. [INAUDIBLE].
